questions.prompt: Complete: "Look at those dark clouds! It ___ rain."
questions.explanation: A prediction based on present evidence (dark clouds) uses "be going to": it is going to rain. "will" is used for predictions without visible evidence or for instant decisions; "is" alone has no main verb; "goes to" is not a future form.

questions.prompt: Complete: "The phone is ringing. Don't worry, I ___ answer it."
questions.explanation: An instant decision made at the moment of speaking uses "will": I'll answer it. "am going to" suggests a plan made earlier, which contradicts the spontaneous decision; "am" needs the -ing form; "answer" alone has no future marker.

questions.prompt: Choose the correct sentence about a fixed arrangement:

options.text: I will meet the dentist at 5 tomorrow.
I am meeting the dentist at 5 tomorrow.
I meet the dentist at 5 tomorrow.
I going to meet the dentist at 5 tomorrow.

questions.explanation: A fixed, arranged plan with another person and a definite time uses the present continuous for the future: I am meeting the dentist at 5 tomorrow. "will" sounds like a prediction or instant decision, not a firm appointment; the present simple (c) is for timetables, not personal arrangements; (d) drops "am" before "going to".

questions.prompt: Complete: "I'll call you when I ___ home."
questions.explanation: After time words like "when" we use the present simple, not a future form, even for future meaning: when I get home. "will get", "am going to get" and "will be getting" all wrongly place a future form inside the time clause.

Timetables and schedules use the present simple, even for the future: the train leaves at 8:45. "will leave" and "is going to leave" treat a fixed timetable like a prediction; "is leaving" suits a one-off arrangement, not a regular schedule.

options.text: Maybe it going to snow tonight.

questions.explanation: An uncertain prediction (signalled by "maybe") naturally uses "will": maybe it will snow tonight. The present continuous (b) implies a fixed arrangement, which is odd for weather; the present simple (c) suggests a habit/timetable; (d) drops "is" before "going to".
